exhibits.title: Welcome to the Museum
exhibits.body:
Welcome to the Museum of Mr. K's Technology — a walk through one teacher's journey with computers, cameras, games, and machines that make things.

Walk with the arrow keys or WASD (or the on-screen pad). Near any display, press E to read about it and F to hear the audio guide.

Six galleries surround the Great Hall. Follow the red carpet and explore in any order!

exhibits.title: Commodore 64
exhibits.body:
Placeholder text. The Commodore 64 was the best-selling single computer model of all time. This is where the story begins: typing BASIC programs out of magazines and saving them to cassette tape.

Replace this text in exhibits.json with the real story of Mr. K's first computer.

exhibits.title: Apple Macintosh 128K
exhibits.body: Placeholder text. The 1984 Macintosh introduced the mouse and the graphical desktop to the mainstream. A text-only exhibit — some displays don't need a picture to tell their story.

exhibits.title: Nintendo Entertainment System
exhibits.body: Placeholder text. The console that revived home video games in the West. Blowing into cartridges was never actually necessary, but everyone did it anyway.

images.caption: Control Deck with the iconic rectangular controller (placeholder illustration).

exhibits.title: Nintendo Game Boy
exhibits.body: Placeholder text. Four shades of green, ran forever on AA batteries, and survived falls that would destroy any modern device. Portable gaming started here.

exhibits.title: Polaroid OneStep
exhibits.body: Placeholder text. Instant photography — press the button, hear the whir, and watch the picture develop in your hand. The original 'share immediately' technology.

images.caption: OneStep with rainbow stripe, mid-eject (placeholder illustration).

exhibits.title: MakerBot Replicator
exhibits.body: Placeholder text. An early desktop 3D printer. A gallery exhibit can show several images side by side — swipe or scroll the strip above. Great for before/after shots of prints, or a build timelapse.

images.caption: Mid-print, filament spool on the left (placeholder illustration).
A finished print on the build plate (placeholder illustration).

exhibits.title: Amazon Echo Dot
exhibits.body: Placeholder text. The moment talking to a computer stopped being science fiction and started being how you set kitchen timers. An early ambassador of everyday AI.

exhibits.title: Windows 95
exhibits.body: Placeholder text. The Start button, the taskbar, and a launch so big it had its own Rolling Stones song. Software in a cardboard box you bought at a store.
